title: The Fed Has Two Tools to Influence Money Market Conditions

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: New York Fed staff researchers Adam Copeland and Owen Engbretson analyze how the Federal Reserve's two policy tools—administrative rates (IORB) and balance sheet size (Fed liabilities)—affect money market conditions using confidential OFR trade-level Treasury repo data. Their main finding is that both tools have significant and independent effects on the liquidity risk premium dealers charge to intermediate funding, with IORB increases and Fed liability decreases both raising the cost of intermediation.
key_points: The Fed has two distinct monetary policy tools: changes to administrative rates (IORB) and changes to balance sheet size (Fed liabilities), which can be adjusted independently.
A one standard deviation increase in IORB (226 basis points) drives up the liquidity risk premium by 2.1 to 3.5 basis points.
A one standard deviation increase in Fed liabilities ($750 billion) drives down the liquidity risk premium by 1.6 to 2.5 basis points.
The average spread dealers charge clients to intermediate funding over the sample period is 7.5 basis points.
Dealers serve as intermediaries in secured funding markets, borrowing from cash-rich investors (money market mutual funds) and lending to levered clients (hedge funds).
The Treasury repo market has an estimated size of over $5 trillion outstanding in the first half of 2024.
IORB increased from 15 to 540 basis points during the 2022-23 tightening cycle; Fed liabilities peaked at nearly $6 trillion in 2021 and fell to $3.5 trillion by December 2024.
A $1 billion change in Fed liabilities has a larger estimated effect on the liquidity risk premium when IORB is low compared to when IORB is high.
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical evidence that the Fed can decouple economic policy from financing conditions. While lowering rates typically loosens economic conditions, the Fed can simultaneously shrink its balance sheet to maintain tighter money market conditions—a critical insight for understanding the Fed's policy flexibility and potential policy tradeoffs in future cycles.
macro_context: The research suggests the Fed can achieve mixed policy outcomes simultaneously: lowering rates to stimulate the economy while using balance sheet reduction to maintain money market discipline. This has implications for understanding the transmission of monetary policy to financial conditions.
market_structure_context: The analysis focuses on repo market structure, specifically how dealer intermediation spreads in Treasury repo are affected by Fed policy. Dealers hold buffers of liquid securities to mitigate risks, and the cost of holding this buffer (liquidity risk premium) varies with the opportunity cost of cash. The OFR centrally cleared repo data captures most interdealer trading and a portion of dealer-to-client trades via the Sponsored Service program.
important_numbers: 226 basis points (one standard deviation change in IORB)
2.1-3.5 basis points (effect on liquidity risk premium from IORB change)
$750 billion (one standard deviation change in Fed liabilities)
1.6-2.5 basis points (effect on liquidity risk premium from Fed liabilities change)
7.5 basis points (average dealer spread to intermediate funding)
notes: Source published same day as TODAY (2026-04-06), indicating very recent research. Content is a blog post summarizing a New York Fed Staff Report. The source body was truncated but the key findings and methodology are captured. The research uses confidential trade-level data and provides quantitative estimates for the independent effects of both Fed policy tools on money market spreads. This is high-quality, source-grounded research from an authoritative central bank source.
